title: The gaseous state: ideal and real gases and pV = nRT
summary: 9701 AS gas laws and the ideal gas equation pV = nRT — with PhET Gas Properties simulation for visualising particle behaviour.
learningObjectives: State the assumptions of the kinetic theory of ideal gases.
Apply pV = nRT with correct units (Pa, m³, K or kPa, dm³).
Combine gas laws: Boyle, Charles, and the ideal gas equation.
Explain deviations from ideal behaviour at high pressure and low temperature.

simpleExplanation.title: Particles in a box
simpleExplanation.summary: Ideal gases have particles that move randomly, collide elastically, and occupy negligible volume. The equation pV = nRT links pressure, volume, amount, and temperature.
steps: At constant T, pressure increases when volume decreases (Boyle's law).
At constant p, volume is proportional to absolute temperature (Charles's law).
Combine into pV = nRT — always use kelvin for T and consistent pressure/volume units.
Real gases deviate when particles are close together (intermolecular forces matter).

sections.content: Gas calculations appear on **every 9701 AS paper**. The most common error is **unit mismatch** — mixing kPa with cm³ without converting. Use the sim to build intuition before tackling past-paper algebra.
pV = nRT | R = 8.31 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ (SI) | At rtp: 1 mol gas ≈ 24 dm³ | T(K) = T(°C) + 273

sections.question: Calculate the mass of CO₂ in a 500 cm³ flask at 100 kPa and 25 °C. (Mr CO₂ = 44.0)

sections.content: Show **conversion to SI** or state clearly if using p in kPa and V in dm³ with an appropriate R value. Cambridge mark schemes often award a conversion mark before the final answer.
